model_narrative: We plan military recruitment over a sequence of years. Each year we decide how many recruits to take on under each available length of enlistment, which in turn determines how many enlisted men are on duty in each year. Recruits cost money to service, with the cost depending on the length of enlistment and scaled by that year's inflation index. The objective is to make the total cost over the whole horizon as small as possible.

sets.name: t
sets.doc: the planning years in chronological order, numbered consecutively from one; year one is the first year of the horizon and each later year follows the one before it

sets.name: l
sets.doc: the available lengths of enlistment, measured in whole years; a recruit signed under a given length serves for exactly that many years starting in the year of recruitment and then leaves

params.name: infl
params.doc: the inflation index applied to costs in each year, a unitless multiplier that scales the base cost of service in that year

params.name: req
params.doc: the minimum number of enlisted men required to be on duty in each year, in men

params.name: clen
params.doc: the base cost of service for a recruit by enlistment length, in dollars per recruit, before applying the year's inflation index

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the number of recruits taken on in a given year under a given enlistment length

vars.name: e
vars.doc: the number of enlisted men on duty in a given year

vars.name: z
vars.doc: the total cost over the whole horizon, in dollars

constraints.name: z
constraints.intent: The total cost gathers the servicing cost of every recruit taken on across the whole horizon. For each year and each length of enlistment, take the number of recruits taken on that year under that length, value them at the base cost of service for that length, and scale by that year's inflation index. Set the total cost equal to the sum of these amounts over all years and all enlistment lengths.

constraints.name: e[i]
constraints.intent: The number of enlisted men on duty in each year must account for who carries over from the year before and the recruits arriving and departing that year. For each year, the men on duty equal the men on duty in the immediately preceding year, plus everyone newly recruited that year across all enlistment lengths, minus everyone whose service ends that year because the length of their enlistment has fully elapsed since they were recruited. In the very first year there is nobody carried over from before, and no one can have departed yet.

constraints.name: e[i]
constraints.intent: Each year must field enough men to meet the demand for that year. For each year, the number of enlisted men on duty must be at least the minimum troop requirement for that year.

query: Personnel is pitching a bigger long-service core across the whole plan — at least sixteen four-year enlistments signed over the ten years combined, summed across every year. What does the optimizer return for total manpower cost?
expected_pyomo: model.long_floor = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x[i, 4] for i in model.t) >= 16)

query: There's a proposal on the table to keep the force from leaning too hard on four-year terms: across the whole horizon, four-year enlistments shouldn't exceed 40% of all recruits signed (that is, total four-year recruits capped at 0.4 times the sum of recruits of every enlistment length). Plug that in and tell me where cost lands.
expected_pyomo: model.long_share = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x[i, 4] for i in model.t) <= 0.40 * sum(model.x[i, j] for i in model.t for j in model.l))

query: The readiness office is pitching a uniform floor: every single year of the plan should carry at least seven troops on strength, not just the years with a high requirement. What does the optimizer return for cost under that across-the-board floor?

query: We're looking at a two-part build-out package: sign at least fifteen recruits in total over the first three years combined, and also keep strength from dropping more than one troop year-over-year anywhere in the plan. Run both together and tell me the cost impact.
expected_pyomo:
model.early_buildup = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x[i, j] for i in [1, 2, 3] for j in model.l) >= 15)
def _smooth(model, i):
    return model.e[i] >= model.e[i - 1] - 1 if i > 1 else Constraint.Skip
model.no_sharp_drop = Constraint(model.t, rule=_smooth)

query: The plan back-loads recruiting and the early years look thin. Shouldn't the first three years together pull in at least fifteen recruits regardless of enlistment length, so we're not scrambling later? What's keeping the model from front-loading like that?
expected_pyomo: model.early_floor = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x[i, j] for i in [1, 2, 3] for j in model.l) >= 15)

query: The mix tilts heavily toward four-year terms and barely touches the cheap one-year recruits. Couldn't we just require that one-year enlistments make up at least a quarter of the one-year-plus-four-year intake over the horizon (one-year recruits at least 0.25 times the sum of one-year and four-year recruits)? What's the tradeoff the model is making by skipping them?
expected_pyomo: model.short_share = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x[i, 1] for i in model.t) >= 0.25 * sum(model.x[i, j] for i in model.t for j in [1, 4]))

query: Some years we sign no long-service recruits at all, which leaves no seasoned core building up. Wouldn't it be prudent to bring on at least one four-year enlistment every single year? What's the model seeing that makes it skip them?

query: Two things bug me about the plan at once: the long-service core stays thin and year 5 sits right at its bare minimum. Couldn't we just hold both lines — at least fourteen four-year enlistments across the horizon and at least six on strength in year 5? Walk me through what's pulling the plan the other way.
expected_pyomo:
model.long_total = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x[i, 4] for i in model.t) >= 14)
model.yr5_ready = Constraint(expr=model.e[5] >= 6)
